html:
Roofing the Cross-Dock and Bonded Warehouse Roofs of Killam Industrial Park

Killam Industrial Park sits inside Laredo's inland-port economy, a few miles from the World Trade Bridge, and its roofs are almost all the same animal: big, flat, and covered in rooftop equipment serving cross-dock and customs-bonded freight operations. We work on these buildings on the schedule the tenant's freight operation allows, not the other way around.

What's Actually Up There

The park's building stock runs from cross-dock distribution centers with dozens of dock-high doors to customs-bonded warehouses holding freight under CBP seal while it clears for the next leg north or south. A growing share of the newer construction is temperature-controlled space added for produce and perishables moving through Laredo rather than dry storage.

Roof age varies more than the buildings' uniform tilt-wall exteriors suggest. Some sections date to the park's earlier build-out and have been recovered or overlaid at least once. Newer tenant-improvement buildings went up with spec-grade TPO, chosen by the developer's cost model rather than by anything the eventual tenant asked for.

Every one of these roofs carries more rooftop equipment than the original as-built shows: smoke hatches and skylights, exhaust fans tied to dock levelers, RTUs serving office and mezzanine space, and on the cold-storage buildings, condensing units and curb-mounted ductwork cutting through the field membrane.

Border Heat Is Harder on These Roofs Than the Warranty Sheet Suggests

Laredo summers run past 100 degrees for weeks at a stretch, and a dark or aging membrane sitting in direct sun can run 40 to 50 degrees hotter than the air around it. That thermal cycling is what splits laps and shrinks membrane at the perimeter long before a "20-year" system gets anywhere near 20 years.

A reflective TPO cap sheet or a silicone recoat over sound modified bitumen brings surface temperatures down and slows that cycling. We look at membrane condition and rooftop mechanical load together before recommending a recover versus a tear-off, rather than defaulting to the more expensive option.

Dock-Level Penetrations and Bonded-Warehouse Add-Ons

CBP-bonded space brings security cameras, motion sensors, and fire-suppression standpipes onto roofs that were never designed for them. Each one is a penetration that needs a manufacturer-approved boot or pitch pan, not a field-fabricated patch that shows up as a callback six months later.

Cold-storage retrofits bring their own problems. Insulation and vapor-retarder requirements for a freezer addition are different from what the original TPO detail called for, and we've reworked curb flashing on more than one building where a mechanical contractor added freezer capacity mid-lease and never touched the membrane correctly around it.

  • Ponding at interior drains where tenant-improvement ductwork went in without regrading the field
  • Split laps along east- and west-facing parapets where afternoon sun hits hardest
  • Pitch pans around security-camera conduit that were never re-sealed after installation
  • Cracked pipe boots at cold-storage condenser lines
  • Membrane pull-away at expansion joints between original construction and later additions
  • Undersized or clogged scuppers on older buildings never designed for today's rooftop equipment load

Scheduling Around a Live Freight Operation

We coordinate with the property manager or tenant's operations lead around dock schedules, stage material off to the side rather than blocking dock doors, and follow bonded-area protocols where crews need background checks and an escort.

An emergency dry-in over a bonded warehouse is a different conversation than a standard building. A wet seal breach over sealed freight can raise issues beyond the roof itself, so our response focuses on getting a temporary seal down fast and documenting roof conditions before and after we touch anything.

Material staging is its own puzzle on a working freight yard. We plan drop zones with the tenant ahead of time so a delivery truck or a lift never sits in a lane a dock crew needs, and we keep our footprint small enough that loading doesn't slow down while we're on site.

Documentation Property Managers and REIT Owners Actually Need

Before work starts we photograph and moisture-scan the affected area, and we write scope tied to specific roof sections or building addresses, which matters when park buildings are held by different ownership entities or split across a REIT portfolio. Warranty transfer paperwork gets handled the same way when a building changes hands or a lease turns over.

We also provide the documentation insurers ask for after a wind or hail event: dated photos, condition notes, itemized scope. We don't guarantee claim outcomes; that decision belongs to the adjuster and the policy.

For a property manager overseeing several buildings in the park at once, we keep records by building so a roof's repair history is easy to pull up the next time a lease turns over or a new owner takes over the asset.

What Killam Park Building Owners Ask Us Most

Does a CBP-bonded warehouse need special roofing precautions?

Yes. Crews need to clear background checks and work with an escort in bonded areas, and we keep a log of who's on the roof each day so the tenant has what they need for their own compliance file.

Can you recover an existing membrane instead of a full tear-off?

Often, if the substrate is dry and sound. We check moisture content and insulation condition first; a recover over wet insulation just traps the problem under a new surface.

How fast can you respond if a roof leak threatens bonded freight?

We prioritize these calls. The first goal is a temporary seal to stop active water intrusion, followed by full documentation of the affected area for the tenant and the building owner.

Who pays for roof work when a building has multiple tenants under one landlord?

That depends on the lease structure, but the roof and membrane are usually the landlord's responsibility. We work directly with the property manager and document square footage by tenant space when cost allocation matters.

Do you handle the newer cold-storage additions differently than dry warehouse space?

Yes. Insulation, vapor retarders, and curb detailing all change once a space goes from ambient to refrigerated, and we account for that in the scope rather than treating it like a standard TPO section.
